premise: A delta city can keep the sea out only by renewing its marriage to the river each flood season, a rite now run by oyster women, dredge crews, and bankrupt officials because the old priestly families sold the wetlands to developers. When the river-spouse demands a human lover from every ward in exchange for one more year of dry ground, the whole city has to decide what bodies count as public infrastructure.
protagonist_descriptor: oyster women, dredge crews, and broke city officials
pressure_descriptor: flood control, debt, and sacrificial civic ritual
why_it_might_work: It has an immediate public problem, a strong collective cast, and a premise that throws sex, class, and ecology into the same negotiations. The ritual creates scene engines in council chambers, mud flats, bedrooms, and levee worksites.
risk: It could tip into allegorical overload if the city never feels materially lived in. The erotic ritual also has to stay specific and politically grounded rather than decorative.

premise: In a volcanic mud-bath town where sulfur vents are said to loosen the dead from the body, the widow of a famous faith healer turns her family's collapsing spa into a walk-in clinic for hauntings. Her sons, daughters, cousins, and exes must log each ghost on county health forms, scrub strangers in medicinal clay, and obey whatever new instructions the grandmother keeps dictating through the pipes.
protagonist_descriptor: a bickering healer family running a haunted bathhouse
pressure_descriptor: inheritance chaos, ghost intake, and seasonal tourist money
setting_descriptor: a desert hot-springs town built on sulfur vents
why_it_might_work: The book can keep generating funny, gross, and metaphysical scenes without losing a concrete business reality. Family labor and paperwork give the supernatural a sturdy daily rhythm.
risk: The tone has to avoid turning cozy or whimsical. Without enough bite in the family dynamics, the hauntings could feel lightweight.

premise: After an avian-flu blast freezer kills half his shift, a Guatemalan ammonia technician discovers that poultry plants across the South share a single white corridor where disappeared workers keep walking. He rides from plant to plant under borrowed papers, taking dirtier contracts each time, because he is certain his vanished sister is somewhere ahead of him in the cold.
protagonist_descriptor: a migrant refrigeration mechanic chasing the vanished
pressure_descriptor: wage theft, border precarity, and a deadly labor obsession
setting_descriptor: poultry plants and labor camps across the American South
why_it_might_work: The labor specificity gives the strangeness real traction, and the moving workplace structure naturally feeds escalation. The search for the sister keeps the engine personal without softening the class pressure.
risk: If the plants blur together, the novel could become monotonous despite the mobility. The uncanny corridor needs rules strong enough to feel inevitable, not random.

premise: After saints begin appearing in bus exhaust and sewer steam, an inland capital creates a Bureau of Manifestations to certify miracles, reroute pilgrim traffic, and settle liability claims when the divine damages public property. One career inspector rises through the bureau over three decades as each apparition averts one disaster while causing three new civic emergencies, until the city has to decide whether salvation is now a municipal service.
protagonist_descriptor: a civil servant inside the miracle bureau
pressure_descriptor: urban governance under recurring divine interventions
setting_descriptor: a sprawling inland capital remade by apparitions
why_it_might_work: The premise offers a durable procedural engine while staying large enough to matter to the whole city. The long span lets policy, faith, and infrastructure reshape one another in visible stages.
risk: The bureaucracy can deaden the magic if every chapter feels like paperwork. The protagonist needs enough authority and compromise to carry the decades.

premise: When the mortgage company schedules the clearance of the last worker tower on a flood-basin archipelago, every apartment begins opening into a different version of the building from a life the residents might have lived. A garment worker, her ex-husband, and their adult children race floor by floor to rescue debt ledgers, recipes, and the belongings of a dead son before the storm surge and the bulldozers erase whichever world turns out to be theirs.
engine_guess: family tragedy in a multiplying tower
protagonist_descriptor: a working-class family trapped in a shifting apartment block
pressure_descriptor: eviction, grief, and collapsing parallel domestic spaces
setting_descriptor: the last labor tower on a flooded archipelago
why_it_might_work: The family objective is simple and brutal, while the structure lets each floor become a fresh emotional and speculative arena. It can stay intimate even as the worldbuilding expands.
risk: The parallel-life device could get mechanically clever at the expense of grief. The book also needs enough external urgency to keep it from becoming purely recursive.

premise: With bees gone, three migrant crews ferry hummingbird drones, pollen baths, and hand-pollination kits from one bloom window to the next, chasing strawberries and orchard fruit from southern Mexico to western Canada. Their contracts forbid local attachments and cross-crew sex, but bodies, visas, and firmware all get traded anyway, especially after a rogue fungal network starts steering the drones toward shrines and graves instead of farms.
protagonist_descriptor: a transnational convoy of pollination workers and drone wranglers
pressure_descriptor: crop deadlines, border movement, and desire on the job
setting_descriptor: the bloom corridor from Mexico to western Canada
why_it_might_work: It has a built-in route, tangible work, and a plural cast whose alliances can keep reshuffling. Ecology, tech, and eros are bound into the same moving system rather than layered on top of one another.
risk: The convoy structure could turn episodic if the relational arcs do not accumulate. The fungal intelligence needs to deepen the book, not just provide weird garnish.

premise: In a city built around a volcano believed to choose the next government by whom it spares, the Office of Last Rites drills constantly for the eruption every calendar says is due. When the mountain opens ahead of schedule and starts naming districts rather than people, the chief mortuary planner has to run corpse recovery, rationing, and sacrificial lotteries while the city argues over whether obedience is piety or murder.
protagonist_descriptor: a chief mortuary planner in a ritual city-state
pressure_descriptor: eruption management, political succession, and sacred triage
setting_descriptor: a crater city governed by volcanic religion
why_it_might_work: The premise joins disaster logistics to theology in a way that can generate relentless public choices. Its world feels legible enough for procedure while remaining strange enough to surprise.
risk: The scale and severity can flatten the cast if too many decisions stay abstract. It also risks becoming uniformly grim unless the civic factions are vividly differentiated.

premise: Across two decades of border privatization, a woman who disinfects the toilets and trauma rooms on transcontinental deportation trains works her way up to route supervisor, learning every coupling, restraint protocol, and sewage system on the line. When her estranged son is loaded aboard under a different country's paperwork, she has one season to sabotage the institution she helped keep running.
pressure_descriptor: bureaucratic survival inside a migrant removal machine
setting_descriptor: transcontinental detention trains crossing a privatized border regime
why_it_might_work: The labor is physical, ugly, and procedural, which gives the institution real weight. The long career arc lets complicity accumulate before the family collision forces a break.
risk: The moral frame has to stay complex enough that the protagonist is more than a symbol of complicity. The setting also demands enough variation to avoid corridor fatigue.

premise: A debt-crushed kelp scraper maintains the storm wall protecting a northern coast, stripping invasive growth from the concrete so the state can keep extending the barrier and moving whole towns inland. At the lowest tides the drowned district where he was born rises intact on the seaward side, and he starts smuggling furniture and fugitives between the tidal streets and the relocation camps following the wall north.
protagonist_descriptor: a storm-wall maintenance worker from a drowned district
pressure_descriptor: debt, forced relocation, and illegal crossings into the tidal dead
setting_descriptor: a migrating storm wall on a cold northern coast
why_it_might_work: It gives one isolated worker a public-scale task and a clear illicit sideline, so the novel can alternate labor scenes with increasingly uncanny crossings. The coast itself becomes a changing opponent.
risk: The solitary mode could narrow the emotional bandwidth if secondary relationships stay thin. The drowned district needs enough concrete texture to feel more than symbolic ruin.

premise: The Quill family make their money dragging up whatever the peat bog coughs back: saints, sheep, war relics, drunken uncles, and the occasional lake bride. When a record heat wave exposes the rib cage of the Mother of Bogs and every retrieval starts bringing up one of their own dead, the family falls back on bad jokes, bait lines, field manuals, and increasingly gruesome salvage work to keep the valley from becoming sacred ground.
protagonist_descriptor: a professional bog-recovery family with too much history
pressure_descriptor: heat-wave exposure, family grief, and mythic containment work
setting_descriptor: a peat valley where the bog returns the dead
why_it_might_work: The retrieval business supplies a dependable procedural spine while the family voice can keep the tone lively under real sorrow. The bog is specific enough to create memorable set pieces over and over.
risk: The comedy has to sharpen the tragedy rather than cushion it. The mythic reveal could feel familiar unless the family trade remains the book's true center of gravity.

premise: A defense startup sells police departments, megachurches, and school boards a bodycam model called SHEPHERD that claims to detect divine intent six seconds before any violent act. The book tracks moderators, chaplains, compliance officers, militia dads, and plaintiffs through leaked Slack threads, deposition clips, bug tickets, and sermon transcripts as every failed prediction becomes either proof of God or grounds for another shooting.
protagonist_descriptor: a chorus of users, auditors, believers, and litigants around one violent platform
pressure_descriptor: algorithmic prophecy colliding with armed institutions
setting_descriptor: a near-future America of cameras, churches, and lawsuits
why_it_might_work: The form can stay fast, plural, and caustic while the premise keeps raising the stakes of interpretation. It has room for real comedy without losing the brutality of its institutions.
risk: The collage could feel cold or overdetermined if the voices do not sharply distinguish themselves. The satire also has to resist becoming a single joke about stupid believers or stupid tech people.

premise: During the final week of the Paris Commune in May 1871, a laundress sewing sandbags for a barricade and an ambulance driver notice that the city's statues have begun stepping down at night to carry children, lovers, and ammunition to safer streets. As Versailles closes in and the dead keep briefly returning to finish ordinary acts of care, the pair help build a household around one doomed block where nobody agrees on what counts as alive, guilty, or a citizen.
protagonist_descriptor: a laundress and an ambulance driver inside a collapsing uprising
pressure_descriptor: street war, civic collapse, and impossible acts of care
setting_descriptor: Paris during the last week of the Commune
why_it_might_work: It puts tenderness inside a violently public event and lets the uncanny sharpen rather than replace the history. The narrow time frame gives the premise urgency while the block-sized household keeps it human.
risk: The historical material has to stay specific enough that the magic does not blur the politics. The tenderness can also curdle into sentimentality if the violence is not faced directly.
